subject: Physics
solution:
Balancing length $l$ will give emf of cell $\therefore E=K l$
Here $\mathrm{K}$ is potential gradient.
If the cell is short circuited by resistance 'R'
Let balancing length obtained be $l^{\prime}$ then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& V=k l^{\prime} \\
& r=\left(\frac{E-V}{V}\right) R \\
\Rightarrow & V=E-V \\
\Rightarrow & 2 V=E \\
\text { or, } & 2 K l^{\prime}=K l \\
\therefore \quad & l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
Power consumed in external resistance R is $P = \frac{E^2 R}{(R + r)^2}$.
To find maximum, differentiate with respect to R:
$\frac{dP}{dR} = E^2 \frac{(R + r)^2 - 2R(R + r)}{(R + r)^4} = E^2 \frac{r - R}{(R + r)^3}$.
$\frac{dP}{dR} = 0$ gives $R = r$.
This is the maximum power transfer theorem: maximum power is delivered when the external load resistance equals the source internal resistance.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the principle of superposition, we analyse the circuit with one source active at a time.

Case 1: Only the $10\text{ V}$ source is active (the $5\text{ V}$ source is replaced by a short circuit).

With the $5\text{ V}$ source shorted, the top and bottom nodes of the bridge are directly connected by a wire, forming a Wheatstone bridge.

Checking for balance:
Left arm ratio: $\dfrac{4}{4} = 1$
Right arm ratio: $\dfrac{2}{2} = 1$

The ratios are equal, so the bridge is balanced. Hence, no current flows through the middle branch connecting the two nodes, and the contribution to $I_2$ from the $10\text{ V}$ source is $0\text{ A}$.

Equivalent resistance of the balanced bridge:
$(4 + 2)\,\Omega \parallel (4 + 2)\,\Omega = 6\,\Omega \parallel 6\,\Omega = 3\,\Omega$

Total resistance including the series $1\,\Omega$ resistor:
$R_{eq} = 3 + 1 = 4\,\Omega$

Current drawn from the $10\text{ V}$ source:
$I_{1(10V)} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5\text{ A}$

This current splits equally between the two $6\,\Omega$ branches, so the current through the bottom $4\,\Omega$ resistor is:
$I_{3(10V)} = \dfrac{2.5}{2} = 1.25\text{ A}$

Case 2: Only the $5\text{ V}$ source is active (the $10\text{ V}$ source is replaced by a short circuit).

Now the $5\text{ V}$ source is applied across the top and bottom nodes, and the $1\,\Omega$ resistor lies in the middle branch between the left and centre nodes.

For the left branch ($4\,\Omega$ and $4\,\Omega$ in series), the potential at the midpoint is $\dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5\text{ V}$.
For the right branch ($2\,\Omega$ and $2\,\Omega$ in series), the potential at the midpoint is also $\dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5\text{ V}$.

Since both midpoints are at the same potential, no current flows through the $1\,\Omega$ middle branch. Hence, the contribution to $I_1$ from the $5\text{ V}$ source is $0\text{ A}$.

Equivalent resistance seen by the $5\text{ V}$ source:
$(4 + 4)\,\Omega \parallel (2 + 2)\,\Omega = 8\,\Omega \parallel 4\,\Omega = \dfrac{8 \times 4}{8 + 4} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,\Omega$

Total current supplied by the $5\text{ V}$ source:
$I_{2(5V)} = \dfrac{5}{8/3} = \dfrac{15}{8} = 1.875\text{ A}$

Current through the left branch (which includes the bottom $4\,\Omega$ resistor):
$I_{3(5V)} = \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625\text{ A}$

Applying superposition:
$I_1 = I_{1(10V)} + I_{1(5V)} = 2.5 + 0 = 2.5\text{ A}$
$I_2 = I_{2(10V)} + I_{2(5V)} = 0 + 1.875 = 1.875\text{ A}$
$I_3 = I_{3(10V)} + I_{3(5V)} = 1.25 + 0.625 = 1.875\text{ A}$

Hence, the correct option is $(1)\ I_1 = 2.5\text{ A},\ I_2 = 1.875\text{ A},\ I_3 = 1.875\text{ A}$.

solution:
Given the total resistance of the wire is R. If the polygon has n numbers of side, then the resistance r, as shown in the following figure, of each side can be written as

r= Rn.................(1)

Among all the resistances, n-1 resistances are connected in series combination, whereas, their equivalent resistance is connected in parallel with the nth resistance. The equivalent circuit diagram for the above set of resistances can be depicted as follows:

From the above equivalent circuit diagram, the net equivalent resistance Req for the entire combination can be calculated as follows-

1Req= 1n-1r+1r= 1n-1Rn+1Rn= nn-1R+nR= n2n-1RReq= n-1Rn2

solution: For $\mathrm{ADE} \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{10}$ or $\quad R^{\prime}=\frac{20 x}{10+2 x}$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{20 \mathrm{x}}{10+2 \mathrm{x}}+20-\mathrm{x}+20-\mathrm{x} $$ or $\frac{20 \mathrm{x}}{10+2 \mathrm{x}}+40=2 \mathrm{x}$ Solving we get $$ \mathrm{x}=10 \Omega $$ Putting the value of $x=10 \Omega$ in equation (i) We get $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}} & =\frac{20 \times 10}{10+2 \times 10}+20-10+20-10 \\ & =\frac{80}{3}=26.7 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
solution:
The wire bent into a circle of radius $r$ has resistance $\lambda \cdot \pi r$ for the semicircular arcs, and the straight wire of length $2r$ has resistance $\lambda \cdot 2r$. The three paths between A and B (two semicircular arcs of the circle and one straight wire through center) are in parallel.

$\frac{1}{R_{AB}} = \frac{2}{\lambda \pi r} + \frac{1}{\lambda \cdot 2r} + \frac{2}{\lambda \cdot 3\pi r}$

$= \frac{1}{\lambda r} \left[\frac{2}{\pi} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{3\pi}\right]$

$= \frac{1}{\lambda r} \left(\frac{12 + 3\pi + 4}{6\pi}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda r} \cdot \frac{16 + 3\pi}{6\pi}$

$R_{AB} = \lambda r \left(\frac{6\pi}{16 + 3\pi}\right) = \frac{6\pi \lambda r}{3\pi + 16}$
